html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirteen — Part Two

The Book of Shemu'el Bet

SHEMU'EL BET

The Second Book of Samuel

Chapters 7 through 12

The Davidic Covenant — Mefiboshet — The Bathsheva Failure — Natan's Indictment

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

4QSama (4Q51) — Cave 4

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTERS 7 THROUGH 12 — THE DAVIDIC COVENANT AND ITS SHADOW

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 7 Dawid's Desire to Build — YHWH's Covenant With Dawid — Your House and Your Kingdom Shall Be Made Sure Forever

1 Now when the king lived in his house and YHWH had given him rest from all his surrounding enemies,

2 The king said to Natan the prophet, See now, I dwell in a house of cedar, but the Ark of Creator dwells in a tent.

3 And Natan said to the king, Go, do all that is in your heart, for YHWH is with you.

4 But that same night the word of YHWH came to Natan,

5 Go and tell my servant Dawid, Thus says YHWH: Would you build me a house to dwell in?

6 I have not lived in a house since the day I brought up the people of Yisra'EL from Egypt to this day, but I have been moving about in a tent for my dwelling.

7 In all places where I have moved with all the people of Yisra'EL, did I speak a word with any of the judges of Yisra'EL, whom I commanded to shepherd my people Yisra'EL, saying, Why have you not built me a house of cedar?

8 Now therefore thus you shall say to my servant Dawid, Thus says YHWH of hosts, I took you from the pasture, from following the sheep, that you should be prince over my people Yisra'EL.

9 And I have been with you wherever you went and have cut off all your enemies from before you. And I will make for you a great name, like the name of the great ones of the earth.

10 And I will appoint a place for my people Yisra'EL and will plant them, so that they may dwell in their own place and be disturbed no more. And violent men shall afflict them no more, as formerly,

11 From the time that I appointed judges over my people Yisra'EL. And I will give you rest from all your enemies. Moreover, YHWH declares to you that YHWH will make you a house.

12 When your days are fulfilled and you lie down with your fathers, I will raise up your offspring after you, who shall come from your body, and I will establish his kingdom.

13 He shall build a house for my name, and I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever.

14 I will be to him a father, and he shall be to me a son. When he commits iniquity, I will discipline him with the rod of men, with the stripes of the sons of men,

15 But my steadfast love will not depart from him, as I took it from Sha'ul, whom I put away from before you.

16 And your house and your kingdom shall be made sure forever before me. Your throne shall be established forever.

17 In accordance with all these words, and in accordance with all this vision, Natan spoke to Dawid.

18 Then King Dawid went in and sat before YHWH and said, Who am I, O YHWH Creator, and what is my house, that you have brought me thus far?

19 And yet this was a small thing in your eyes, O YHWH Creator. You have spoken also of your servant's house for a great while to come, and this is instruction for mankind, O YHWH Creator.

20 And what more can Dawid say to you? For you know your servant, O YHWH Creator.

21 Because of your promise, and according to your own heart, you have brought about all this greatness, to make your servant know it.

22 Therefore you are great, O YHWH Creator. For there is none like you, and there is no Creator besides you, according to all that we have heard with our ears.

23 And who is like your people Yisra'EL, the one nation on earth whom Creator went to redeem to be his people, making himself a name and doing for them great and awesome things by driving out before your people, whom you redeemed for yourself from Egypt, a nation and its gods?

24 And you established for yourself your people Yisra'EL to be your people forever. And you, O YHWH, became their Creator.

25 And now, O YHWH Creator, confirm forever the word that you have spoken concerning your servant and concerning his house, and do as you have spoken.

26 And your name will be magnified forever, saying, YHWH of hosts is Creator over Yisra'EL, and the house of your servant Dawid will be established before you.

27 For you, O YHWH of hosts, the Creator of Yisra'EL, have made this revelation to your servant, saying, I will build you a house. Therefore your servant has found courage to pray this prayer to you.

28 And now, O YHWH Creator, you are Creator, and your words are true, and you have promised this good thing to your servant.

29 Now therefore may it please you to bless the house of your servant, so that it may continue forever before you. For you, O YHWH Creator, have spoken, and with your blessing shall the house of your servant be blessed forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 7. The Davidic covenant — the Nathan oracle — is the theological center of the entire Shemu'el narrative and one of the most significant covenant declarations in the Nevi'im. Verse 16 — your house and your kingdom shall be made sure forever — establishes the covenant foundation for the entire Davidic dynasty.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. Dawid's prayer — verses 18-29 — is one of the most sustained acts of covenant humility in the Nevi'im. Who am I — verse 18 — and what is my house? The king who has just received the greatest covenant promise in the Nevi'im responds with astonished gratitude rather than satisfaction.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves the Nathan oracle with some variants from the Masoretic. Verse 14 — I will be to him a father and he shall be to me a son — is the foundational covenant statement of the father-son relationship between YHWH and the Davidic king, which structures the entire Tehillim theology of kingship.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5-7 — would you build me a house? I have not lived in a house since Egypt. Did I ever ask for a house of cedar? YHWH's rhetorical questions invert the entire project: Dawid intended to do something for YHWH. YHWH declares he will do something for Dawid — I will make you a house, verse 11. The covenant direction of blessing flows from YHWH to Dawid, not from Dawid to YHWH. Verse 12-16 — the Davidic covenant — eternal dynasty, father-son relationship, steadfast love that will not depart, kingdom established forever. The contrast with Sha'ul is explicit: my steadfast love will not depart from him as I took it from Sha'ul. This is the covenant promise that sustains every Davidic king through every failure.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 8 Dawid's Victories — He Made a Name For Himself — YHWH Gave Victory Wherever He Went

1 After this Dawid defeated the Pelishtim and subdued them, and Dawid took Meteg-ha-Ammah out of the hand of the Pelishtim.

2 And he defeated Moav and he measured them with a line, making them lie down on the ground. Two lines he measured to be put to death, and one full line to be kept alive. And Moav became servants to Dawid and brought tribute.

3 Dawid also defeated Hadadezer the son of Rekhov, king of Tzovah, as he went to restore his power at the river Euphrates.

4 And Dawid took from him one thousand seven hundred horsemen and twenty thousand foot soldiers. And Dawid hamstrung all the chariot horses but left enough for a hundred chariots.

5 And when the Syrians of Damascus came to help Hadadezer king of Tzovah, Dawid struck down twenty-two thousand men of the Syrians.

6 Then Dawid put garrisons in Aram-Damascus, and the Syrians became servants to Dawid and brought tribute. And YHWH gave victory to Dawid wherever he went.

7 And Dawid took the shields of gold that were carried by the servants of Hadadezer and brought them to Yerushalayim.

8 And from Betakh and from Berotai, cities of Hadadezer, King Dawid took very much bronze.

9 When Toi king of Khamath heard that Dawid had defeated the whole army of Hadadezer,

10 Toi sent his son Yoram to King Dawid, to ask about his health and to bless him because he had fought against Hadadezer and defeated him — for Hadadezer had often been at war with Toi. And Yoram brought with him articles of silver, of gold, and of bronze.

11 These also King Dawid dedicated to YHWH, together with the silver and gold that he dedicated from all the nations he subdued,

12 From Edom, Moav, the Ammonites, the Pelishtim, Amalek, and from the spoil of Hadadezer the son of Rekhov, king of Tzovah.

13 And Dawid made a name for himself when he returned from striking down eighteen thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt.

14 Then he put garrisons in Edom — throughout all Edom he put garrisons — and all the Edomites became Dawid's servants. And YHWH gave victory to Dawid wherever he went.

15 So Dawid reigned over all Yisra'EL. And Dawid administered justice and equity to all his people.

16 Yoav the son of Tzeruya was over the army, and Yehoshafat the son of Akhilud was recorder,

17 And Tzadok the son of Akhituv and Akhimelekh the son of Evyatar were priests, and Seraya was secretary,

18 And Benaya the son of Yehoyada was over the Kerethites and the Pelethites, and Dawid's sons were priests.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 8. The summary of Dawid's military victories — verse 6 and 14 — repeats the covenant formula YHWH gave victory to Dawid wherever he went. The military catalog is a covenant document: every victory is attributed to YHWH, every dedication of spoil returns it to YHWH.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verse 15 — Dawid administered justice and equity to all his people — is read as the covenant definition of the ideal king. The warrior king who defeats external enemies also administers internal covenant justice. The two functions of the covenant monarch are inseparable.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 8 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 13 — Dawid made a name for himself — is the fulfillment of YHWH's promise in Chapter 7:9 — I will make for you a great name. YHWH makes the name; the battles are the instrument.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 and 14 — YHWH gave victory to Dawid wherever he went. The twofold repetition of this formula brackets the entire military catalog. Dawid's victories are not products of military genius alone. The covenant king who inquires of YHWH before battle — Chapter 5 — receives YHWH's active support in battle. Verse 15 — justice and equity to all his people. The administrative list that follows — verse 16-18 — names the covenant officers who sustain the kingdom. Dawid's strength is not personal centralization but covenant delegation: military commander, recorder, priests, secretary, bodyguard.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 9 Dawid Honors His Covenant With Yonatan — Mefiboshet — He Shall Eat at My Table

1 And Dawid said, Is there still anyone left of the house of Sha'ul, that I may show him kindness for Yonatan's sake?

2 Now there was a servant of the house of Sha'ul whose name was Tziva. And when they called him to Dawid, the king said to him, Are you Tziva? And he said, I am your servant.

3 And the king said, Is there not still someone of the house of Sha'ul, that I may show the kindness of Creator to him? Tziva said to the king, There is still a son of Yonatan — he is crippled in his feet.

4 The king said to him, Where is he? And Tziva said to the king, He is in the house of Makhir the son of Ammiel, at Lo-Devar.

5 Then King Dawid sent and brought him from the house of Makhir the son of Ammiel, at Lo-Devar.

6 And Mefiboshet the son of Yonatan, son of Sha'ul, came to Dawid and fell on his face and paid homage. And Dawid said, Mefiboshet. And he answered, Behold, I am your servant.

7 And Dawid said to him, Do not fear, for I will show you kindness for the sake of your father Yonatan, and I will restore to you all the land of Sha'ul your father, and you shall eat at my table always.

8 And he paid homage and said, What is your servant, that you should show regard for a dead dog such as I?

9 Then the king called Tziva, Sha'ul's servant, and said to him, All that belonged to Sha'ul and to all his house I have given to your master's grandson.

10 And you and your sons and your servants shall till the land for him and shall bring in the produce, that your master's grandson may have bread to eat. But Mefiboshet your master's grandson shall always eat at my table. Now Tziva had fifteen sons and twenty servants.

11 Then Tziva said to the king, According to all that my lord the king commands his servant, so will your servant do. So Mefiboshet ate at Dawid's table, like one of the king's sons.

12 And Mefiboshet had a young son, whose name was Mikha. And all who lived in Tziva's house became Mefiboshet's servants.

13 So Mefiboshet lived in Yerushalayim, for he ate always at the king's table. Now he was lame in both his feet.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 9. The restoration of Mefiboshet is the fulfillment of the covenant between Dawid and Yonatan in Shemu'el Aleph 20:15 — do not cut off your steadfast love from my house forever. The covenant sworn before YHWH between two living men is honored by the survivor for the son of the dead.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verse 7 — do not fear, for I will show you kindness for the sake of your father Yonatan — is read as the covenant model of hesed — steadfast covenant love — extended beyond death to the descendants of the covenant partner.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 9 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The phrase kindness of Creator — verse 3 — is the covenant term hesed Elohim — the faithfulness that belongs to YHWH's own character. Dawid extends YHWH's own quality of covenant loyalty to Mefiboshet.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — is there still anyone left of the house of Sha'ul, that I may show him kindness for Yonatan's sake? The question is the covenant act itself. Dawid is not fulfilling a legal obligation under compulsion — he is actively seeking the descendant of his covenant partner to show him hesed. Verse 7 — you shall eat at my table always. The crippled son of the dead prince — who describes himself as a dead dog — is given the seat of a king's son at the royal table. The covenant of Dawid and Yonatan, sworn in the wilderness when both were fugitives and enemies of the same king, produces this: a dead man's son seated at the living king's table forever.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 10 The Ammonite Insult — Dawid Defeats the Ammonites and the Syrians

1 After this the king of the Ammonites died, and Khanun his son reigned in his place.

2 And Dawid said, I will deal loyally with Khanun the son of Nakhash, as his father dealt loyally with me. So Dawid sent by his servants to console him concerning his father. And Dawid's servants came into the land of the Ammonites.

3 But the princes of the Ammonites said to Khanun their lord, Do you think, because Dawid has sent comforters to you, that he is honoring your father? Has not Dawid sent his servants to you to search the city and to spy it out and to overthrow it?

4 So Khanun took Dawid's servants and shaved off half the beard of each and cut off their garments in the middle, at their hips, and sent them away.

5 When it was told Dawid, he sent to meet them, for the men were greatly ashamed. And the king said, Remain at Yerikho until your beards have grown and then return.

6 When the Ammonites saw that they had become a stench to Dawid, the Ammonites sent and hired the Syrians of Beit-Rekhov and the Syrians of Tzovah, twenty thousand foot soldiers, and the king of Ma'akhah with one thousand men, and the men of Tov, twelve thousand men.

7 And when Dawid heard of it, he sent Yoav and all the host of the mighty men.

8 And the Ammonites came out and drew up in battle array at the entrance of the gate, and the Syrians of Tzovah and of Rekhov and the men of Tov and Ma'akhah were by themselves in the open country.

9 When Yoav saw that the battle was set against him both in front and in the rear, he chose some of the best men of Yisra'EL and arrayed them against the Syrians.

10 The rest of his men he put in the charge of Avishai his brother, and he arrayed them against the Ammonites.

11 And he said, If the Syrians are too strong for me, then you shall help me, but if the Ammonites are too strong for you, then I will come and help you.

12 Be of good courage, and let us be courageous for our people, and for the cities of our Creator, and may YHWH do what seems good to him.

13 So Yoav and the people who were with him drew near to battle against the Syrians, and they fled before him.

14 And when the Ammonites saw that the Syrians fled, they also fled before Avishai and entered the city. Then Yoav returned from fighting against the Ammonites and came to Yerushalayim.

15 But when the Syrians saw that they had been defeated by Yisra'EL, they gathered themselves together.

16 And Hadadezer sent and brought out the Syrians who were beyond the Euphrates. They came to Khelam, with Shovakh the commander of the army of Hadadezer at their head.

17 And when it was told Dawid, he gathered all Yisra'EL together and crossed the Yarden and came to Khelam. The Syrians arrayed themselves against Dawid and fought with him.

18 And the Syrians fled before Yisra'EL, and Dawid killed of the Syrians the men of seven hundred chariots, and forty thousand horsemen, and wounded Shovakh the commander of their army, so that he died there.

19 And when all the kings who were servants of Hadadezer saw that they had been defeated by Yisra'EL, they made peace with Yisra'EL and became subject to them. So the Syrians were afraid to save the Ammonites anymore.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 10. The Ammonite insult — shaving half the beards and cutting the garments of Dawid's envoys — is a deliberate covenant violation. Dawid sent the envoys to honor the treaty relationship established with Khanun's father. The insult triggers the war that will provide the context for the Bathsheva narrative in Chapter 11.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full. Verse 12 — be of good courage for our people and for the cities of our Creator, and may YHWH do what seems good to him — is read as Yoav's covenant battle cry: the fight is for the covenant people and the covenant places, and the outcome is in YHWH's hands.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 10 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The covenant dignity of Dawid's envoys is violated by the Ammonites. Dawid's response — wait at Yerikho until your beards grow — is the covenant honor he shows to his servants before responding militarily.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — Dawid said, I will deal loyally with Khanun the son of Nakhash, as his father dealt loyally with me. Dawid's covenant initiative is entirely honorable. He sends comfort to a bereaved king. The Ammonite princes' suspicion — he sent spies — inverts covenant generosity into political threat. This is the covenant pattern that follows Dawid's every generous act in the post-Chapter 7 narrative: good intentions produce unexpected conflict. Verse 12 — may YHWH do what seems good to him. Yoav, who has shown covenant limitation throughout Shemu'el, here speaks pure covenant theology: fight for YHWH's people and leave the outcome to YHWH.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 11 Dawid and Bathsheva — The Death of Uriyah — The Thing Dawid Had Done Displeased YHWH

1 In the spring of the year, the time when kings go out to battle, Dawid sent Yoav, and his servants with him, and all Yisra'EL. And they ravaged the Ammonites and besieged Rabbah. But Dawid remained at Yerushalayim.

2 It happened, late one afternoon, when Dawid arose from his couch and was walking on the roof of the king's house, that he saw from the roof a woman bathing. And the woman was very beautiful.

3 And Dawid sent and inquired about the woman. And one said, Is not this Bathsheva, the daughter of Eli'am, the wife of Uriyah the Khittite?

4 So Dawid sent messengers and took her, and she came to him, and he lay with her. Now she had been purifying herself from her uncleanness. Then she returned to her house.

5 And the woman conceived, and she sent and told Dawid, I am pregnant.

6 So Dawid sent word to Yoav, Send me Uriyah the Khittite. And Yoav sent Uriyah to Dawid.

7 When Uriyah came to him, Dawid asked how Yoav was doing and how the people were doing and how the war was going.

8 And Dawid said to Uriyah, Go down to your house and wash your feet. And Uriyah went out of the king's house, and there followed him a present from the king.

9 But Uriyah slept at the door of the king's house with all the servants of his lord, and did not go down to his house.

10 When they told Dawid, Uriyah did not go down to his house, Dawid said to Uriyah, Have you not come from a journey? Why did you not go down to your house?

11 Uriyah said to Dawid, The Ark and Yisra'EL and Yehudah dwell in booths, and my lord Yoav and the servants of my lord are camping in the open field. Shall I then go to my house, to eat and to drink and to lie with my wife? As you live, and as your soul lives, I will not do this thing.

12 Then Dawid said to Uriyah, Remain here today also, and tomorrow I will send you back. So Uriyah remained in Yerushalayim that day and the next.

13 And Dawid invited him, and he ate in his presence and drank, so that he made him drunk. And in the evening he went out to lie on his couch with the servants of his lord, but he did not go down to his house.

14 In the morning Dawid wrote a letter to Yoav and sent it by the hand of Uriyah.

15 In the letter he wrote, Set Uriyah in the forefront of the hardest fighting, and then draw back from him, that he may be struck down, and die.

16 And as Yoav was besieging the city, he assigned Uriyah to the place where he knew there were valiant men.

17 And the men of the city came out and fought with Yoav, and some of the servants of Dawid among the people fell. Uriyah the Khittite also died.

18 Then Yoav sent and told Dawid all the news about the fighting.

19 And he instructed the messenger, When you have finished telling all the news about the fighting to the king,

20 Then, if the king's anger rises, and if he says to you, Why did you go so near the city to fight? Did you not know that they would shoot from the wall?

21 Who killed Avimelekh the son of Yeruba'al? Did not a woman cast an upper millstone on him from the wall, so that he died at Tevetz? Why did you go so near the wall? then you shall say, Your servant Uriyah the Khittite is dead also.

22 So the messenger went and came and told Dawid all that Yoav had sent him to tell.

23 The messenger said to Dawid, The men gained an advantage over us and came out against us in the field, but we drove them back to the entrance of the gate.

24 Then the archers shot at your servants from the wall. Some of the king's servants are dead, and your servant Uriyah the Khittite is dead also.

25 Dawid said to the messenger, Thus shall you say to Yoav, Do not let this matter trouble you, for the sword devours now one and now another. Strengthen your attack against the city and overthrow it. And encourage him.

26 When the wife of Uriyah heard that Uriyah her husband was dead, she lamented over her husband.

27 And when the mourning was over, Dawid sent and brought her to his house, and she became his wife and bore him a son. But the thing that Dawid had done displeased YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 11. The Bathsheva narrative is the central covenant failure of the Dawid narrative. Verse 1 — the time when kings go out to battle — Dawid remained at Yerushalayim. The covenant failure begins with absence from covenant responsibility. The king who should be at war is on his roof at dusk.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 11 in full. Uriyah's refusal to go home while the Ark and Yisra'EL dwell in booths — verse 11 — is one of the most profound covenant contrasts in the Nevi'im: the Khittite soldier displays greater covenant loyalty than the Yisra'ELite king.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 11 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 27 — the thing that Dawid had done displeased YHWH — is the covenant judgment stated plainly and without elaboration at the end of the chapter. The entire sequence — adultery, deception, murder by proxy — is gathered into a single covenant declaration.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — Uriyah said, the Ark and Yisra'EL and Yehudah dwell in booths — shall I then go to my house to eat and drink and lie with my wife? The Khittite — not an Israelite by birth — speaks the language of covenant solidarity. His refusal to take the comfort Dawid is trying to give him exposes the king's moral bankruptcy with every word. Dawid then has the man who refuses to go home sent to his death by his own hand — carrying his own death warrant. The covenant irony is complete. Verse 27 — but the thing that Dawid had done displeased YHWH. The chapter closes with the covenant judgment. Nothing is hidden from YHWH. The private transaction on the roof of the palace is fully visible to the one who made the palace possible.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 12 Natan's Parable — You Are the Man — The Child Dies — Shelomoh Born — Rabbah Taken

1 And YHWH sent Natan to Dawid. He came to him and said to him, There were two men in a certain city, the one rich and the other poor.

2 The rich man had very many flocks and herds,

3 But the poor man had nothing but one little ewe lamb, which he had bought. And he brought it up, and it grew up with him and with his children. It used to eat of his morsel and drink from his cup and lie in his arms, and it was like a daughter to him.

4 Now there came a traveler to the rich man, and he was unwilling to take one of his own flock or herd to prepare for the guest who had come to him, but he took the poor man's lamb and prepared it for the man who had come to him.

5 Then Dawid's anger was greatly kindled against the man, and he said to Natan, As YHWH lives, the man who has done this deserves to die,

6 And he shall restore the lamb fourfold, because he did this thing, and because he had no pity.

7 Natan said to Dawid, You are the man. Thus says YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, I anointed you king over Yisra'EL, and I delivered you out of the hand of Sha'ul.

8 And I gave you your master's house and your master's wives into your arms and gave you the house of Yisra'EL and of Yehudah. And if this were too little, I would add to you as much more.

9 Why have you despised the word of YHWH, to do what is evil in his sight? You have struck down Uriyah the Khittite with the sword and have taken his wife to be your wife and have killed him with the sword of the Ammonites.

10 Now therefore the sword shall never depart from your house, because you have despised me and have taken the wife of Uriyah the Khittite to be your wife.

11 Thus says YHWH, Behold, I will raise up evil against you out of your own house. And I will take your wives before your eyes and give them to your neighbor, and he shall lie with your wives in the sight of this sun.

12 For you did it secretly, but I will do this thing before all Yisra'EL and before the sun.

13 Dawid said to Natan, I have sinned against YHWH. And Natan said to Dawid, YHWH also has put away your sin — you shall not die.

14 Nevertheless, because by this deed you have utterly scorned YHWH, the child who is born to you shall die.

15 Then Natan went to his house. And YHWH afflicted the child that Uriyah's wife bore to Dawid, and he became sick.

16 Dawid therefore sought Creator on behalf of the child. And Dawid fasted and went in and lay all night on the ground.

17 And the elders of his house stood beside him, to raise him from the ground, but he would not, nor did he eat food with them.

18 On the seventh day the child died. And the servants of Dawid were afraid to tell him that the child was dead, for they said, Behold, while the child was yet alive, we spoke to him, and he did not listen to us. How then can we say to him the child is dead? He may do himself some harm.

19 But when Dawid saw that his servants were whispering together, Dawid understood that the child was dead. And Dawid said to his servants, Is the child dead? They said, He is dead.

20 Then Dawid arose from the earth and washed and anointed himself and changed his clothes. And he went into the house of YHWH and worshiped. He then went to his own house. And when he asked, they set food before him, and he ate.

21 Then his servants said to him, What is this thing that you have done? You fasted and wept for the child while it was alive, but when the child died, you arose and ate food.

22 He said, While the child was still alive, I fasted and wept, for I said, Who knows whether YHWH will be gracious to me, that the child may live?

23 But now he is dead. Why should I fast? Can I bring him back again? I shall go to him, but he will not return to me.

24 Then Dawid comforted his wife Bathsheva, and went in to her and lay with her, and she bore a son, and he called his name Shelomoh. And YHWH loved him

25 And sent a message by Natan the prophet. So he called his name Yedidyah, because of YHWH.

26 Now Yoav fought against Rabbah of the Ammonites and took the royal city.

27 And Yoav sent messengers to Dawid and said, I have fought against Rabbah — moreover, I have taken the city of waters.

28 Now then gather the rest of the people together and encamp against the city and take it, lest I take the city and it be called by my name.

29 So Dawid gathered all the people together and went to Rabbah and fought against it and took it.

30 And he took the crown of their king from his head. The weight of it was a talent of gold, and in it was a precious stone, and it was placed on Dawid's head. And he brought out the spoil of the city, a very great amount.

31 And he brought out the people who were in it and set them to labor with saws and iron picks and iron axes and made them toil at the brick kilns. And thus he did to all the cities of the Ammonites. Then Dawid and all the people returned to Yerushalayim.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 12. The Nathan parable — verse 1-6 — is one of the most perfectly constructed covenant indictments in the Nevi'im. The prophet does not accuse directly. He tells a story that causes Dawid to pronounce his own judgment. Verse 7 — you are the man — is the covenant moment the parable was building toward.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 12 in full. Dawid's response after the child's death — verse 20 — arising, washing, going to worship, then eating — is read as the covenant model of grief and acceptance. He wept while there was hope. When YHWH's decision was final, he arose and worshiped. Submission to YHWH's will is the covenant response to unanswerable loss.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 12 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 13 — Dawid said, I have sinned against YHWH — is the shortest and most complete covenant confession in the Nevi'im. No excuses, no context, no mitigation. Three words in the Hebrew: khata'ti la-YHWH — I have sinned against YHWH.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7-9 — you are the man — I anointed you — I delivered you — I gave you — if this were too little I would add to you more — why have you despised the word of YHWH? YHWH's covenant indictment reviews his own gifts to Dawid in order to make the covenant violation more terrible: Dawid had everything and took the one thing that was not his. Verse 22-23 — Dawid's explanation of his fast and its end is covenant realism of a high order. While there was possibility, he pressed YHWH. When the decision was made, he accepted it. He does not collapse into permanent mourning. He worships, eats, comforts Bathsheva. The covenant person who wrestles fully with YHWH also submits fully to his verdict.

END OF PART TWO — CHAPTERS 7 THROUGH 12
